items.q: What force does a rope in a tug-of-war experience?
items.correct: Tension - it is being pulled at both ends.
items.misconception: Compression - because it is squeezed by hands.
items.why: Students confuse the localised hand grip with the force through the rope. Pulling stretches the rope - that's tension. Compression would be pushing the ends together (which a rope cannot resist).

items.correct: Producing a specific quantity of identical items, then switching to make another batch.
items.why: Students confuse batch with mass production. Batch produces FIXED QUANTITIES with the line then changing - between one-off (single item) and mass (continuous high volume).

items.q: What property do shape memory alloys have?
items.correct: They return to a pre-set shape when heated.
items.misconception: They remember every shape they have ever been bent into.
items.why: Students think SMAs are infinitely programmable. They actually have ONE 'remembered' shape, set during high-temperature treatment. Heating restores that shape; the alloy doesn't store a history of deformations.

items.q: Whose 2005 Double Diamond models the design process?
items.why: Correct answer: UK Design Council. “IDEO” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which technique systematically alters product attributes?
items.why: Correct answer: SCAMPER. “TRIZ” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many diamonds are in the Double Diamond model?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 diamonds. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
